exemplar.title:
To what extent will the increase in property prices per m2 
affect the urbanization of the city Skopje?

detailedExemplarData.title:
To what extent will the increase in property prices per m2 
affect the urbanization of the city Skopje?

subject.title: Geography
subject.description:
**IB Geography** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **physical and human geography, environmental interactions, global challenges, and spatial patterns**, with a strong focus on **fieldwork, data analysis, case studies, and Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Geographic themes - structured questions | 2h 15min | 2h 15min | SL: 35% / HL: 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Geographic perspectives - data response and essays | 1h 15min | 1h 15min | SL: 40% / HL: 45% |
| **Paper 3** | Global interactions - extended essay responses | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Fieldwork investigation (2,500 words) | 20 hours | 20 hours | SL: 25% / HL: 20% |

strengths: Fieldwork question is clearly geographical and linked to IB Option G content
Specific municipality (Aerodrom) is identified, enabling primary data collection
Multiple maps provide basic spatial orientation
weaknesses: Maps lack essential cartographic conventions (scale bar, north arrow, source)
Fieldwork question remains broad and lacks explicit theoretical framework citation
Spatial focus shifts between whole city and Aerodrom
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work meets the minimum standards of a geographical question and place specificity (Band 2) but falls short in depth, precision and cartographic professionalism. While the question and location enable primary data collection, the broad scope and missing conventions justify a low Band 2âlevel score reduced to 1 to reflect overall quality and to accommodate the total-marks constraint.
feedback.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

strengths: Methods are described with clear relevance to the research question
Correct data-collection techniques (online survey, official statistics) were used
Actual primary data (120 responses) and secondary time-series prices were collected
weaknesses: Sampling strategy rationale and bias-control measures are omitted
Survey design lacks justification and pilot-testing information
No evidence of data triangulation or reliability checks
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The methods are relevant and correctly implemented (Band 2) but lack comprehensive explanation and methodological rigor. Important details on sampling, bias control and questionnaire design are missing. A score of 1 reflects this moderate performance and fits the scaled distribution for a total of 8.

strengths: Most data presented are directly relevant to the research question
Appropriate presentation techniques (pie charts, column charts, time-series graphs) are employed
Conventions (captions, units) are present in many figures
weaknesses: Numerous presentation errors: missing axis labels, misnumbered captions, inconsistent citation style
No quantitative green-space measures; reliance on qualitative description
Bilingual labels reduce readability and some graphics overlap text
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data relevance and presentation are generally appropriate (Band 3), but frequent errors and omissions weaken the treatment. The work warrants 2 marks out of 6 to reflect competent but flawed execution, aligned with the need to scale the overall total to 8.
feedback.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

strengths: Observed trends are linked to demand and green-space concepts
Anomalies are acknowledged within the narrative
weaknesses: Analysis is predominantly descriptive with no statistical testing
Key gaps remain (green-space quantification, bias assessment)
Completeness is limited by missing quantitative evidence and deeper interpretation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The analysis shows basic interpretation and trend-linking (lower end of Band 3-4) but lacks statistical rigor and full evidence. A scaled score of 1 reflects minimal analytical depth while maintaining the required total mark.

strengths: A clear, fully supported conclusion references how property prices influence urbanisation
Conclusion directly follows from analysis and addresses the research question
weaknesses: Conclusion is brief and ends abruptly mid-sentence
Lacks quantified support and detailed summary of key numerical findings
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is coherent and fully supported (Band 2) but is underdeveloped in depth and precision. It merits full marks for the criterion, contributing appropriate weight to the total of 8.

strengths: Evaluation outlines relevant strengths (combined methods) and weaknesses (data gaps, bias)
Improvement suggestions are pertinent (municipal records, quantitative green-space data)
weaknesses: Explanation of how improvements would impact reliability and validity is superficial
Depth of evaluation is limited and lacks comprehensive discussion
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation meets Band 2 requirements with relevant but brief suggestions. To align with the total-marks constraint and reflect moderate performance, the criterion is awarded 1 mark.

strandSelections.reasoning: Several maps are supplied: a national Balkans map, a map of Skopjeâs municipalities and an annotated sketch of Aerodrom. Basic orientation is therefore provided. Yet many maps lack full cartographic conventions (scale bars, north arrows, source citations) and contain pixelated images or hand-drawn legends that reduce clarity. This places the work in Band 2 â maps are present but not fully professional.

The fieldwork question is clearly geographical, focusing on the relationship between property-price change and urbanisation in Skopje, an issue anchored in IB Option G: Urban Environments. The student explicitly mentions relevant concepts such as housing demand, land-use patterns and green-space loss, and then derives a hypothesis. However, the theoretical underpinning remains fairly general (no specific urban-land-rent models or bid-rent theory are cited) and the wording of the question is still very broad (âthe city Skopjeâ rather than a single municipality). This aligns best with Band 2, where the question is geographical and syllabus-linked but not yet underpinned by a fully articulated theoretical framework allowing sophisticated hypothesis testing.

The student employs pie charts, column charts and two time-series graphs, all generally appropriate to the data type. However, several graphics are duplicated, contain bilingual labelling that is difficult to read, or lack precise titles and axes, reducing overall effectiveness. Therefore the work reaches Band 3 (appropriate techniques) but not Band 5-6 (most effective).

Data were actually collected: 120 survey responses and time-series price data. The techniques (online survey, extraction of official statistics) are correctly used, but the student acknowledges sampling bias and limited respondent diversity. No evidence of triangulation or systematic accuracy checks is given. Reliability is therefore moderate, matching Band 2 (correct techniques with minimal but acceptable data).

The analysis is predominantly descriptive: percentages from pie charts are quoted and related qualitatively to rising demand. No statistical tests (e.g., correlation or chi-square) are performed and no confidence levels are calculated. Nevertheless, the descriptive techniques applied are appropriate and accurate, earning the top of Band 1-2 (2 marks).

The student consistently links observed trends (smaller dwelling sizes, newer buildings, price escalation) to the fieldwork question and to concepts such as demand and green-space loss. Some anomalies (e.g., willingness to relocate) are mentioned but not explored statistically. This corresponds to Band 3-4, with 3 marks reflecting competent but not in-depth interpretation.

Skopje is located within North Macedonia, key municipalities are named and Aerodrom is singled out as the core study area. Background information on migration, housing demand and green-space change establishes why the location is suitable for primary data. Nonetheless, the spatial focus wavers (whole city vs Aerodrom) and socio-economic context is brief. These features satisfy Band 2 (specific location enabling data collection) but fall short of the precision and depth required for Band 3.

The evaluation paragraph outlines relevant strengths (combined methods) and weaknesses (lack of green-space data, survey bias). Discussion is concise but shows understanding of methodological impact, fitting Band 2 (appropriate outline) rather than Band 3 (comprehensive explanation).

Most of the dataâhousing price trends, census figures and survey responsesâdirectly address the research question and allow partial analysis. Nonetheless, there is an admitted gap on quantitative green-space measures, and the surveyâs demographic skew limits sufficiency. This positions the strand at the lower end of Band 3-4, so 3 marks are awarded.

A clear conclusion is stated, directly referencing how property prices influence urbanisation and green-space change; it is supported by the preceding discussion. Although brief and lacking quantified corroboration, the linkage is sufficient for the full 2 marks available in Band 2.

Important facets (green-space quantification, bias assessment) are missing, leaving noticeable gaps. Still, the student provides a multi-paragraph narrative that draws on both primary and secondary data. Hence the strand meets Band 1-2 and merits 2 marks.

The student explains a Google Forms survey and describes its broad purpose plus secondary sources from the State Statistical Office. While the links to the research question are explicit, details about sampling strategy, questionnaire design rationale, pilot testing or bias control are absent. Thus the description is clear but not comprehensive, fitting Band 2.

Figure captions and units are usually present, yet many charts omit axis labels, scales, and full legends; map conventions are incomplete. Errors are occasional rather than pervasive, matching the descriptor for Band 3-4. A mid-band score of 3 is justified.

Two workable improvement suggestions are offered: accessing municipal records for verification and collecting quantitative green-space data. The student briefly explains how these would increase reliability. This satisfies Band 2, as suggestions are relevant but not elaborated in depth.

annotations.comment: Geographical context discussion lacks specific socio-economic data for Aerodrom; include demographic trends or income distributions to justify the choice of this municipality.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Map of North Macedonia is missing essential cartographic conventions (north arrow, scale bar, data source). Add these to improve spatial clarity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The fieldwork question directly engages IB Option G content on urban environments, establishing syllabus relevance effectively.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The question is too broad, referencing âcity Skopjeâ rather than a precise area, and lacks explicit linkage to a theoretical urban land-rent model. Refine scope and cite a specific framework (e.g., bid-rent theory).
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The description of theoretical underpinning remains general; explicitly link the investigation to a defined urban model (e.g., bid-rent or von ThÃ¼nen) to strengthen hypothesis formation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The spatial focus shifts between Skopje as a whole and Aerodrom; clarify the precise study area to ensure consistency in data collection and analysis.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The hypothesis clearly links variables (price per mÂ², urban development, green-space loss) in a concise statement focused on Aerodrom.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Ensure table conventions (header alignment, gridlines, clear cell borders) to improve presentation and reader comprehension.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The caption labels the census table as âPhoto 1,â which is misleading. Correct the label to âTableâ and align numbering.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Method description omits sampling strategy rationale and bias-control measures for the Google Forms survey. Detail sampling frame and survey design to bolster reliability.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: Survey table lists questions but lacks justification for each item and potential for leading responses; provide rationale and pre-test outcomes for improved validity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: Pilot the survey and report on feedback; specify how modifications improved question clarity and reduced bias.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: English and Macedonian entries in the table are intermingled, reducing readability. Present each language separately or use clear dual-column formatting.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Footnote citations are inconsistently formatted and lack author or date detail; adopt a uniform citation style (e.g., APA) for clarity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Time-series graph lacks axis labels with units and scale markers, impeding precise interpretation of price changes over time.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The euro symbol is incorrectly rendered as â\epsilon.â Use the standard â¬ symbol to avoid confusion.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Include an assessment of the reliability and limitations of secondary data from the State Statistical Office to contextualize its relevance.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: No quantitative data on green-space loss is presented; incorporate GIS or spatial analysis to measure green-area changes over time.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Consider integrating satellite imagery or land-use maps to quantify green-space reduction and strengthen the environmental analysis.
annotations.groupName: Criterion F: Evaluation

annotations.comment: Map 3 of Aerodrom lacks a north arrow, scale bar, and source citation; add these conventions to improve cartographic professionalism.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Pie chart 2 includes bilingual labels but lacks a clear legend; separate languages or provide distinct legends for readability.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Survey charts omit sample size (n); include this information on each graphic to contextualize percentage results.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Figure captions are numbered inconsistently (Pie chart 11,12,13 appear out of sequence); standardize numbering to avoid confusion.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Improvement suggestions describe general enhancements but lack detail on how changes will affect reliability or validity; specify impact of each proposed action.
annotations.groupName: Criterion F: Evaluation

annotations.comment: Bibliography entries are inconsistently formatted and lack a standardized style; adopt a single referencing format (e.g., APA) for all sources.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Ensure each reference includes author, publication date, title, and access date in a consistent style to improve academic rigour.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Pie chart 14 overlaps with adjacent text and is difficult to read; adjust layout or resize elements to improve legibility.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Clarify chart labels and ensure text does not overlap graphic areas; this will enhance reader comprehension.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Analysis relies solely on descriptive statistics; consider applying correlation or regression to test the relationship between price and other variables.
annotations.groupName: Criterion D: Written Analysis

annotations.comment: The student effectively connects percentage findings (e.g., apartment versus house living) to the narrative, demonstrating clear data interpretation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion D: Written Analysis

annotations.comment: The conclusion ends abruptly mid-sentence and lacks quantified support; finalize statements and reference key numerical findings for a cohesive closure.
annotations.groupName: Criterion E: Conclusion
